Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Wednesday night, a low pressure system is tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies/Texas panhandle area. By Thursday morning (12Z), the 48-hr prog shows a surface low near the Kansas/Oklahoma area with a cold front draped back to the southwest, and that system is going to be the main story for your flight. The cold front looks to be clearing or right on top of your route by your 16Z departure.

Wednesday evening, pull up the latest GFS/NAM and check two things: (1) how fast that low is moving northeast, and (2) where the cold front is going to be sitting at 15-17Z Thursday. If the front is lagging and still hanging over central Texas at your departure time, you've got a problem. If it's already through Austin and pushing into Oklahoma, you're likely in the clear post-frontal.

What would flip this to a no-go: Front stalls over central Texas, or any convective activity fires up along the frontal boundary within 100nm of KEDC. Also watch for any SIGMET issuance for icing over the Rockies — that's your arrival environment.

Leaving KEDC — Departure at 2026-03-19 16:00Z

This is the part I'd watch most carefully. The 48-hr prog (Thu 12Z) shows the frontal boundary pushing through central Texas, with the low tracking toward the Missouri/Kansas area. By 16Z Thursday, you're likely right in the post-frontal slot at Austin, but it could be close. Post-frontal at the surface in central Texas in March usually means:

  • Northwest winds, gusty — think 15-25 knots, possibly more right behind the front
  • Improving visibility but potentially scattered-to-broken post-frontal clouds at 3,000-6,000 ft
  • Decent VFR conditions, but check for any stratus or low cloud layers that linger after frontal passage

The QPF charts show no meaningful precip over central Texas by Thursday, which is good — the moisture is either ahead of you (already east) or the system isn't generating much rain in your departure area. KEDC at 16Z Thursday should be workable, but verify frontal timing the morning of. If the front is slow and still sitting over Austin at departure time, you could be looking at IFR ceilings, rain, and wind shear on climbout. That's a hold-on-the-ground situation.

Climbing out northwest toward Amarillo, you'll be crossing from post-frontal Texas into the drier western atmosphere. Expect the ride to smooth out once you're clear of the frontal zone, probably by the time you're over the Hill Country or West Texas.

The Ride at 10,000 ft

Icing Analysis

This is the big concern on this route. You're flying 10,000 ft MSL from Austin to Denver in mid-March, and you've got a frontal system moving through. The terrain rises significantly — by the time you're approaching the Palmer Divide and the front range near KBJC, terrain is already 5,000-6,000 ft MSL, which puts you only 4,000-5,000 ft above the ground.

No FIP (Forecast Icing Product) charts were provided, so I'm working from the surface prog and QPF data. Here's my read:

Altitude Icing Probability Notes
Below 8,000 ft MSL (TX/NM) Low Post-frontal, drying out, below freezing level likely not an issue
8,000–12,000 ft MSL (NM/CO) Moderate to High Frontal clouds, moist post-frontal flow, temps near or below 0°C — this is your risk zone
Above 12,000 ft MSL Lower, but cold Colder temps mean less SLD risk but still structural icing in clouds

In mid-March over eastern Colorado and the front range, freezing level at 10,000 ft is a real possibility. Post-frontal northwest flow over the Rockies often produces orographic cloud decks with embedded icing that extends well above your cruise altitude. The QPF charts show moisture/precip over the Great Lakes and upper Midwest from this system, and the secondary moisture return could clip the front range of Colorado.

Worst icing band: Likely 8,000–11,000 ft over eastern New Mexico and southeastern Colorado — right where you'll be at cruise altitude on the second half of this flight.

Escape route: Down is your friend here. Dropping to 6,000-7,000 ft over the eastern plains of Colorado gets you below the main cloud bases in post-frontal flow. Do NOT try to climb above it at 12,000-14,000 ft in a standard GA piston without oxygen and known-ice certification — you'll likely find more icing, not less.

SLD risk: Can't be ruled out without FIP data, but post-frontal northwest flow over the Rockies in March with temps near freezing is a classic SLD setup. Check the PIREP database and FIP charts the morning of before you go. Any SLD indication is a hard no-go at 10,000 ft on this route.

Turbulence & Ride Quality

No GTG (Graphical Turbulence Guidance) charts were provided, but here's what the pattern tells me:

  • Central Texas to Amarillo: Post-frontal, northwest winds — expect light to moderate chop, especially over the escarpment terrain between 4,500 and 7,000 ft MSL. At 10,000 ft you're above most of the mechanical turbulence from the terrain features. Should be manageable.
  • Amarillo to Colorado: This is where the ride gets interesting. Northwest flow behind a cold front accelerating over the Rockies generates mountain wave turbulence. Expect moderate chop, possibly isolated moderate turbulence over the Raton Mesa and Trinidad area (KAMA to KBJC leg). Wave activity can reach 10,000 ft and above on this route.
  • Descent into KBJC: Mountain wave on final approach to the front range airports can produce significant wind shear and turbulence below 10,000 ft. KBJC is at 5,673 ft MSL — you've only got about 4,300 ft of altitude to work with on descent. Check PIREPS for wave activity.

No winds aloft data was provided for this briefing. Strong northwest flow post-frontal at 10,000 ft over the southern plains could give you a 20-40 knot tailwind component on the Austin–Amarillo leg, then shift to a direct crosswind or slight headwind as you turn more northward toward Denver. Check 9,000 and 12,000 ft winds aloft (FD) for your departure time — this will significantly affect fuel planning.

Other Hazards

Hazard Risk Level Leg What to Expect
Frontal passage timing at KEDC HIGH Departure Front may still be on or near Austin at 16Z — verify timing morning of
Structural icing in cloud MODERATE-HIGH NM/CO segment Post-frontal clouds over eastern Rockies, temps near freezing at 10,000 ft
Mountain wave turbulence MODERATE Raton Pass / KBJC approach Northwest flow behind front — expect moderate chop, check PIREPs
Low ceilings / IFR at KBJC LOW-MODERATE Arrival Post-frontal clouds may produce low ceilings on front range — check TAF day-of
Gusty crosswinds at KBJC MODERATE Arrival Post-frontal northwest winds can be 20-35G45 on the front range — this is normal but plan for it

Getting Into KBJC

KBJC (Rocky Mountain Metro) at 16Z+flight time Thursday afternoon — you're looking at mid-afternoon arrival, probably 20-22Z Thursday depending on your cruise speed and winds. The 60-hr prog (valid Fri 00Z) shows the low well to the northeast and the front moving east, with high pressure beginning to build back in from the west over Colorado. That's actually the trend you want.

However, the front range in March behind a cold frontal passage often produces:

  • Strong, gusty northwest winds — 20-30 knots sustained, gusts to 40+ are not unusual at KBJC post-frontal. KBJC's dominant runway is 29L/11R. Northwest winds may favor 29 with a direct crosswind or slight tailwind component on 11 — check the ATIS and be ready for a challenging approach.
  • Scattered post-frontal clouds — likely 3,000-5,000 ft AGL, but could be lower if the front is slow to clear. VFR is probable but not guaranteed.
  • Terrain-induced turbulence below 8,000 ft on the approach from the east

Good alternates: KDEN (Denver International) is your primary alternate — full ILS approaches, 24/7 operations, and better approach options in mountain wave conditions. KAPA (Centennial Airport) works too and is just south of KBJC. KFNL (Northern Colorado) is another option to the north if conditions are better away from the front range urban corridor.

Block time estimate: KEDC to KBJC direct is roughly 700nm. At 10,000 ft with post-frontal northwest component, figure 3.5–4.5 hours depending on your aircraft and actual winds aloft. If you're routing through KAMA as a fuel stop, add an hour on the ground plus flight time.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — with real conditions to respect.

This is flyable but it's not a casual trip. Here's the honest assessment:

Hard stops that would make this a NO-GO:

  • Front still sitting on Austin at 16Z Thursday — you're not punching through a frontal boundary in a piston at 10,000 ft. Wait it out.
  • Any SLD icing PIREPs or forecasts over eastern New Mexico or Colorado
  • KBJC winds exceeding your personal crosswind limits (which can easily be 30G45 post-frontal)
  • Any convective SIGMETs along the route at departure time

What makes this doable:

  • The frontal system is moving out — timing is your friend if the front clears Austin by 14-15Z
  • Post-frontal air over the central plains at 10,000 ft is generally clear with good visibility
  • No meaningful QPF shown over your route from central Texas to Colorado — the heavy moisture stayed east
  • The biggest challenge is the last 200nm over the Rockies terrain, and you can mitigate that with PIREPs and altitude awareness

I wouldn't be nervous about this trip if the front clears Austin on schedule. I would be very nervous if it's running 2-3 hours late.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Wednesday night check: Pull the 00Z model runs (GFS/NAM) and verify frontal position at 18Z Thursday. If models are showing the front over central Texas at 18Z, call this trip and push to Friday morning.
  2. Thursday morning (10Z-12Z): Check the KEDC METAR, TAF, and any AIRMETS/SIGMETs issued overnight. If frontal timing looks good (front clearing by 14-15Z), you're on. Also pull the FIP charts for icing along the route — specifically 8,000-12,000 ft over NM and CO.
  3. Fuel stop at KAMA: Seriously consider stopping at Amarillo Rick Husband (KAMA). It's about 400nm from Austin, lets you get a current PIREP picture from pilots inbound from the north, refuel with known quantities, and check the actual Colorado icing picture before committing to the Raton Pass/front range segment. KAMA has full FBO services.
  4. Altitude strategy: File 10,000 ft but be willing to drop to 7,000-8,000 ft over NM/CO if icing is encountered. Do NOT try to climb out of icing toward 12,000-14,000 ft on a westbound/northbound heading near the Rockies without confirmed clear-air above.
  5. Departure timing: If the front is clearing early, push departure to 14Z-15Z rather than waiting until 16Z — get out ahead of any afternoon heating that could fire post-frontal convection in Texas or resurrect frontal activity.
  6. File IFR even if you expect to be VFR most of the way. Post-frontal cloud decks over the Rockies can develop fast and you want the infrastructure in place.
  7. Alternates filed: Put KDEN as your filed alternate for KBJC. Know KAPA and KFNL as options in your back pocket.
  8. Wind check on Friday: If this all feels marginal Wednesday night, know that Friday is shaping up cleaner with high pressure building — that's a solid backup day.
FlightWeatherWatch · Charts: NOAA/NWS WPC + AWC · Analysis: Claude claude-sonnet-4-6 · NOT FOR FLIGHT PLANNING — obtain an official preflight weather briefing before departure.
